explicit_text: I grew up in Santa Maria, California, where I went to high school with my husband. He graduated in the summer of 1941, and I graduated in December 1941, just before Pearl Harbor. Our families lived in this agricultural valley, and it was a close-knit community. My father-in-law was a prominent community leader there, involved with the Nihonjin-kai and well known to local police.

explicit_text: Pearl Harbor was attacked on December 7th, 1941, just as I was graduating high school. That night, my father-in-law was attending a Japanese community dinner in Los Angeles when the FBI and police came looking for him. The news of the bombing was shocking, and my father-in-law even saw newspaper extras about it when he was driven to LA. It marked the beginning of a difficult time for our family and community.

explicit_text: After the initial evacuation, we were first sent to an assembly center in Tulare, California. It was the first stop before being moved to the more permanent camps. From Tulare, my husband and I were sent to different locations, starting the long separation during the war years.

explicit_text: I was sent to Gila River War Relocation Center in Arizona after Tulare, along with my mother and siblings. My husband Jordan was there with his mother and sister, while his father was separated in Montana. Later, after his father was released, the family reunited in Ogden, Utah. Gila was one of the first camps where we experienced separation and hardship.

explicit_text: We were sent to Crystal City, Texas in April 1943, where we stayed for two and a half years. The camp was unique because it housed people from Hawaii, mainland U.S., Peru, and even some Germans who were American Bund members. It was a diverse community, and my family worked in various jobs like the grocery store and laundry. Crystal City was where we experienced a mix of cultures and the long wait during the war.
